mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW Z4 3.0i / 3.0si engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned Z4 3.0i / 3.0si engine (M54B30 or N52B30) typically costs £1,800–£4,200 supply only depending on which generation and variant you have. Used M54B30/N52B30 engines typically cost £2,000–£2,800 depending on condition and history. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,400. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW Z4 3.0i / 3.0si engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which engine generation your Z4 has. The M54 (2003–2005) is considered 'bomb proof as far as reliability is concerned', though VANOS seals and the DISA valve are known weak points. The N52 (2006–2008) is 'a real gem' and 'probably the zenith of BMW straight sixes', but oil leaks and electric water pump failure are common. The 3.0si is the most desirable variant.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW Z4 3.0i / 3.0si?

acceptedAnswer.text: On the M54 (2003–2005), the most common issues are VANOS seal wear (causing loss of low‑end torque) and DISA valve failure (which can cause catastrophic intake damage). On the N52 (2006–2008), common problems include oil leaks (valve cover, oil filter housing, valvetronic motor gasket), electric water pump failure, and a rare but expensive VANOS oil pressure issue.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Z4 3.0i / 3.0si engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a tidy £5,000–£12,000 Z4, a £1,800–£4,200 recon is 15–50% of the car's value – often worth it on well‑maintained examples. The E86 Z4 Coupe is becoming a modern classic, so well‑preserved examples may justify the investment. Get a full diagnostic assessment before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Z4's engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The E85 Roadster (2003–2005) uses the M54B30 – check for a 3.0‑litre engine with 231 PS. The E85 Roadster or E86 Coupe (2006–2008) with 218 PS is the N52B30 (3.0i). The E85 Roadster or E86 Coupe (2006–2008) with 265 PS is the N52B30 (3.0si). The engine code is stamped on the engine block and listed on your V5C.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair a Z4 3.0i / 3.0si engine issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most issues – M54 VANOS seal wear, DISA valve failure, N52 oil leaks, water pump failure – are repairable. However, if the N52 has suffered the rare VANOS oil pressure issue and the inlet bearing ledge is worn, a new cylinder head assembly (approximately £4,000) or a used engine may be required. If the timing chain has snapped (rare on these engines), a full engine replacement is usually more cost‑effective than a rebuild.

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the Z4 3.0i / 3.0si pricing. Used M54B30/N52B30 engines typically cost £2,000–£2,800 depending on condition and history, with professionally rebuilt units ranging from £2,800–£3,600 with a comprehensive 12‑month warranty. A full engine replacement with labour can cost £6,500–£8,500.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Z4 3.0i / 3.0si engine typically takes 10‑14 hours at £75‑£110/hr (independent BMW specialist). The engine's longitudinal mounting in the front of the vehicle provides relatively good access compared to transverse layouts.

problems.title: M54 VANOS Seal Wear & DISA Valve Failure (2003–2005, M54B30)
problems.symptoms: Loss of low‑end torque, sluggish acceleration, rough idle, hesitation, poor fuel economy, engine warning light, rattling from the intake manifold.
problems.repairCost: £400–£1,000 [PLACEHOLDER] (VANOS seal replacement, DISA valve replacement or reinforcement).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored DISA failure can cause catastrophic intake damage).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if performance issues or rattling are noted.

problems.recommendation: The M54's VANOS system uses rubber seals that degrade over time, causing a loss of low‑end torque and sluggish response. The DISA valve (which adjusts intake runner length) uses a plastic flap that can break, sending debris into the intake manifold — potentially causing catastrophic engine damage. Replacing VANOS seals with upgraded versions and reinforcing or replacing the DISA valve with a metal unit are essential preventative measures. If you hear rattling from the intake manifold, have the DISA valve inspected immediately.

problems.title: N52 Oil Leaks — Valve Cover & Oil Filter Housing (2006–2008, N52B30)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, low oil level warning, visible oil leak from the top or front of the engine, engine warning light.
problems.repairCost: £300–£900 [PLACEHOLDER] (valve cover gasket, oil filter housing gasket, or valvetronic motor gasket replacement).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored oil loss can lead to engine damage).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if oil leak is detected.

problems.recommendation: The N52 is known for oil leaks from the valve cover gasket, oil filter housing, and valvetronic motor gasket. The valve cover itself can warp, requiring full replacement. If the oil filter housing gasket fails, oil can leak onto the serpentine belt, causing it to slip or break — which can then be ingested into the engine through the front crankshaft seal, causing catastrophic failure. A £300–£900 gasket replacement is far cheaper than the consequences of a snapped belt. Replacing the valvetronic motor gasket preventatively is recommended.

problems.title: N52 Electric Water Pump Failure (2006–2008, N52B30)
problems.symptoms: Overheating, coolant loss, engine warning light, poor heater performance, whining noise from the engine bay — the electric water pump can fail without warning.
problems.typicalMileage: 60,000–80,000 miles (can fail as early as 60,000 miles or as late as 135,000 miles).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored overheating can cause head gasket failure).

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you see overheating, stop driving immediately.

problems.recommendation: The N52 uses an electric water pump that will fail with little or no warning. When it fails, the engine overheats rapidly — and in an aluminium‑block engine, that can mean a warped head or blown head gasket. Many owners replace the water pump and thermostat preventatively. A Pierburg replacement pump (the OE supplier) costs around £250, while BMW charges twice as much for the same part. If you notice any overheating or hear a whining noise, have the cooling system inspected immediately.

problems.title: N52 VANOS Oil Pressure Issue (2006–2008, N52B30)
problems.symptoms: Engine warning light (VANOS codes), loss of power, rough running, ticking or chattering noise from the engine.
problems.typicalMileage: 80,000–150,000 miles (varies — not common, but expensive when it occurs).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if VANOS codes appear or unusual noises are heard.

problems.recommendation: The N52's VANOS system uses steel oil sealing rings at the front of the camshafts. These can wear a groove into the alloy cam carrier, causing oil pressure loss and VANOS malfunction. If the inlet bearing ledge is worn, a new cylinder head assembly is required at a cost of approximately £4,000. The exhaust bearing ledge is replaceable at around £365 plus 4‑5 hours of labour. A light chattering noise often sounds like a piston issue but is actually wear in the cam carriers. Regular oil changes (every year or 8,000 miles with fully synthetic oil) are critical to prevent this issue. While rare, this is the most expensive potential repair on the N52.

problems.title: N52 Hydraulic Valve Adjuster (Lifter) Noise (2006–2008, N52B30)
problems.symptoms: Ticking or tapping noise from the engine, particularly when cold or at idle.
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage (issue was corrected on many cars early in their life under warranty).

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: if the noise is present only when cold and disappears when warm, it's an annoyance rather than a catastrophic issue. If it persists, book an inspection.

problems.recommendation: The N52 can suffer from hydraulic valve adjuster (lifter) noise, particularly on cold starts. This issue was corrected on many cars early in their life under warranty. If the noise is present only when cold and disappears when warm, it is more of an annoyance and should not result in catastrophic engine failure. However, if it persists, have the lifters inspected. You can ask a BMW dealer to check the service history using the VIN to confirm if the issue was addressed.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: BMW Z4 3.0i / 3.0si — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential — a broken DISA can destroy the engine
Depends on damage — may require a new head assembly or used engine
Full engine replacement often more cost‑effective

buyingChecks.title: Buying This BMW Z4 3.0i / 3.0si? Check These First
buyIf: * Full BMW service history with documented oil changes (every year or 8,000 miles)
* 2003–2005 (M54): VANOS seals and DISA valve inspected or replaced
* 2006–2008 (N52): Water pump and thermostat replaced preventatively, valve cover gasket addressed
* The 3.0si Coupe is a modern classic in the making
avoidIf: * 2003–2005 (M54): Any sign of intake rattling (DISA valve failure risk)
* 2006–2008 (N52): Any sign of oil leaks or overheating
* 2006–2008 (N52): Persistent lifter noise or VANOS codes
* No evidence of regular oil changes — both engines are intolerant of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The BMW Z4 3.0i and 3.0si represent the pinnacle of the E85/E86 Z4 range — powered by two of BMW's finest naturally aspirated straight‑six engines. The M54 (2003–2005) is "bomb proof" with known but manageable VANOS and cooling system issues. The N52 (2006–2008) is "a real gem" and "probably the zenith of BMW straight sixes", though it has its own quirks — oil leaks, electric water pump failure, and a rare but expensive VANOS oil pressure issue. The 3.0si is "the sweet spot in the Z4 range in terms of bang for buck". Buy on service history above all else — a well‑maintained Z4 3.0i or 3.0si is a rewarding and increasingly collectable car; a neglected example can be expensive to repair.

cta.label: → Compare Z4 3.0i / 3.0si engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW Z4 3.0i / 3.0si (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start — Any ticking, rattling, or tapping? (lifter noise or VANOS warning)
2. Service History — Regular oil changes (yearly or 8,000 miles) — this is non‑negotiable
3. VANOS Health — M54: Any loss of low‑end torque? (VANOS seal wear)
4. DISA Valve — M54: Any rattling from the intake? (DISA valve failure risk)
5. Oil Leaks — Check under the vehicle and around the valve cover and oil filter housing (N52)
6. Water Pump — N52: Has the electric water pump been replaced?
7. Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any VANOS or misfire codes?
8. MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures or any advisory notes
